title: Week 3 Day 3 (1/2) — Tokenizers
summary: Part 1 of 2 · Token vs token ID · special tokens · one tokenizer per model.

sections.heading: What a tokenizer does
sections.remember: Models only see **numbers**. Tokenizer: **text → tokens → token IDs** (and reverse for decoding).
points: **Token** = chunk (subword, word piece) — like colored spans in Week 1 UI.
**Token ID** = integer index in vocab (people often say "token" loosely).
Strict: token = piece · token ID = number.

sections.heading: Special tokens
sections.remember: Reserved IDs (BOS, EOS, chat headers) get meaning because **training data** always used them that way — not hard-coded slots in the transformer.
points: Example: ID 10 at start of every prompt → model learns "new prompt follows".
Different models use different special token schemes.

sections.heading: One tokenizer per model
sections.remember: Never mix tokenizer A with model B — each release ships its own vocab and chat format.
points: Course examples: Llama, Phi, DeepSeek, Qwen — each has its own tokenizer.
Slightly different token counts per tokenizer is normal; don't obsess for cost.
**Tokens ≠ embeddings:** IDs go in; **embedding layer** maps ID → vector (e.g. 2048-dim) deeper in the net.

sections.heading: Base vs instruct models
sections.remember: **Base** = general continuation. **Instruct / chat** = fine-tuned on system/user/assistant turns.
points: Chat models need **`apply_chat_template`** — base models don't expect OpenAI JSON.

cheatSheet: Special tokens = learned from training patterns
Match tokenizer to model · base ≠ instruct
